concepts.name: Graph
concepts.description: A graph is a non-linear data structure consisting of nodes (vertices) and edges.

concepts.name: Node/Vertex
concepts.description: A node, also known as a vertex, is a fundamental unit from which graphs are formed.

concepts.name: Edge
concepts.description: An edge is a connection between two nodes (vertices) in a graph.

concepts.name: Directed Edge
concepts.description: A directed edge is an edge with a specific direction, indicating a one-way relationship from a source node to a destination node.

concepts.name: Dependency
concepts.description: In a directed graph, dependency means one node's completion or processing is required before another node can be processed, typically indicated by a directed edge.

concepts.name: Topological Sort
concepts.description: Topological sort is an algorithm for ordering the vertices of a directed acyclic graph (DAG) such that for every directed edge U to V, U comes before V in the ordering.

concepts.name: Topological Order
concepts.description: The topological order is the linear ordering of vertices produced by a topological sort, respecting all directed dependencies.

concepts.name: Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG)
concepts.description: A Directed Acyclic Graph is a directed graph that contains no cycles, making it suitable for topological sorting.

concepts.name: Cyclic Graph
concepts.description: A cyclic graph is a graph that contains at least one cycle, meaning there is a path from a vertex back to itself.

concepts.name: Time Complexity
concepts.description: Time complexity is a measure of the amount of time required by an algorithm to run as a function of the length of the input.

concepts.name: Linear Time Complexity (V+E)
concepts.description: Linear time complexity O(V+E) indicates that the algorithm's runtime grows proportionally to the sum of the number of vertices (V) and edges (E) in the graph.

concepts.name: DFS (Depth First Search)
concepts.description: Depth First Search is a graph traversal algorithm that explores as far as possible along each branch before backtracking.

concepts.name: BFS (Breadth First Search)
concepts.description: Breadth First Search is a graph traversal algorithm that explores all the neighbor nodes at the present depth before moving on to the nodes at the next depth level.

concepts.name: Visited/Unvisited Nodes
concepts.description: Nodes in a graph can be marked as visited or unvisited during traversal to prevent redundant processing and infinite loops.

concepts.name: Stack
concepts.description: A stack is a linear data structure that follows the Last-In, First-Out (LIFO) principle, commonly used in DFS implementations.

prerequisites.rationale: Nodes are fundamental components of a graph.
Edges are fundamental components of a graph.
A directed edge is a specific type of edge.
Dependency is defined by the direction of edges between nodes.
Topological sort is fundamentally about ordering based on dependencies.
Topological order is the result of performing a topological sort.
A DAG is a specific type of directed graph.
Topological sort can only be applied to a Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG).
Cyclic graphs are introduced as a contrast to DAGs where topological sort is not possible.
Linear time complexity is a specific instance of general time complexity.
O(V+E) is the typical time complexity of topological sort algorithms.
DFS is presented as one method to implement topological sort.
BFS is presented as another method to implement topological sort.
Stacks are a common data structure used in the implementation of DFS-based topological sort.
Tracking visited and unvisited nodes is crucial for any graph traversal algorithm like DFS.
Tracking visited and unvisited nodes is crucial for any graph traversal algorithm like BFS.
